message: The resolve command requires a finding <id> but the contract specifies no discovery affordance for it. Scope item 1 persists an id into findings.jsonl and item 3 consumes it, yet no scope item or acceptance criterion provides a way to surface finding ids (no list command, and no requirement that the blockers_open termination output include them). The end-to-end resolution flow has an unspecified first step.

The ledger-append-failure-after-resolution-append behavior is defined in Assumptions but has no corresponding acceptance criterion or test, so a stated behavior is left ungated. The test-coverage criterion enumerates resolution success, unresolved-blocker failure, hash invalidation, required flags, artifact writing, ledger event writing, and malformed/missing artifacts, but omits the partial-failure path.

Under fingerprint dedup, the waiver summary's required fields are ambiguous. The summary must show 'each waived finding id, reason, and by,' but other criteria require deduplicating by fingerprint when multiple resolution records exist for one fingerprint (possibly with differing reason/by/id) or when multiple current findings share a fingerprint. The contract does not specify which record's id/reason/by populates the single deduped line, so that output is not deterministically verifiable.

AC9 requires a 'loud' waiver summary; 'loud' is subjective and not machine-checkable. The checkable substance is the waived count plus each finding's id, reason, and by. Tests should assert those concrete substrings rather than loudness so the criterion has a deterministic pass condition.

Several fully testable acceptance behaviors are not represented in the enumerated Tests section: fingerprint-vs-id re-emit clearing (AC11), retain-all-records with waiver dedup-by-fingerprint (AC12), multiple findings cleared by one resolution (AC13), unknown-id/non-blocking-id rejection (AC3), and the ledger-payload omission of reason/by (AC4). These are the regression-prone, subtle cases; recommend the Tests list explicitly cover them so the test plan matches the acceptance set. Not a checkability defect — each is expressible as a deterministic temp-dir test today.

The mandated test enumeration (Tests section and the 'Helper-process or temp-directory tests cover…' acceptance criterion) omits the fingerprint-cross-run-match and dedup-by-fingerprint behaviors that other acceptance criteria require (re-emitted finding with same fingerprint/different id stays cleared; waiver summary dedups by fingerprint; multiple findings sharing a fingerprint cleared by one resolution; assumption: 'matches by FINGERPRINT, NOT by finding id'). Because no mandated test exercises these, `go test ./...` / `make check` would still pass for an id-based or non-deduplicating implementation — the validation is vacuous for this load-bearing behavior. Add these cases to the required test list so the gate fails on wrong fingerprint-matching/dedup output.

The write semantics of contract/reviewer/findings.jsonl (truncate-per-run vs append-only) are never stated, yet the approval gate's 'absent from current findings = fixed' path depends on it being latest-run-only. An executor could reasonably make it append-only to mirror the adjacent append-only resolutions.jsonl, which would silently break the 'fixed clears the blocker' path while still passing the temp tests.

The 'current contract hash/version' is treated as a known canonical value but its definition (what content it covers) and the requirement that resolve-time and approve-time use a single shared computation are left implicit. Resolution invalidation — a core gate — depends on this; independent or divergent hash computations would either invalidate every resolution immediately or mis-size the 'any change invalidates' scope.

Cross-run resolution matching relies on the fingerprint (normalized lens/category/message) being stable when a non-deterministic reviewer rephrases the same blocker on a later run. This stability assumption is never surfaced; the helper/temp tests reuse identical strings, so the real-world risk (LLM rephrasing yields a different normalized message -> different fingerprint -> resolution no longer matches) is neither stated nor tested.
